items.content: Ezra Jin Mingri, who founded Beijing’s Zion Church, was detained in October in one of China’s largest crackdowns on a single church in decades.

items.content: A Chinese pastor has been released from jail after Donald Trump intervened to get him freed, in what his family have described as a "miracle".

items.title: Founder of prominent underground church released from prison in China
items.standfirst: Ezra Jin, founder of Zion Church, lands in the US and reunites with family who are ‘overwhelmed with joy’The founder of one of China’s most prominent underground churches has been released from prison and reunited with his family in the United States.Ezra Jin, the founder of Zion Church, landed in the US on Friday evening. He was one of dozens of church members who were detained in a sweeping crackdown on Christians in October. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Historic World Cup furor at 'incomprehensible' FIFA decision to let U.S. forward Balogun play

items.title: Historic World Cup furor at 'incomprehensible' FIFA decision to let U.S. forward Balogun play
items.standfirst: The Belgian soccer federation is challenging FIFA's ruling to let Balogun play despite getting a red card in his previous game. Belgium's statement came just 11 hours before kickoff in Seattle. European soccer body UEFA criticizes FIFA's "incomprehensible and unjustifiable decision."
items.content: The Belgian soccer federation is challenging FIFA's ruling to let Balogun play despite getting a red card in his previous game. Belgium's statement came just 11 hours before kickoff in Seattle. European soccer body UEFA criticizes FIFA's "incomprehensible and unjustifiable decision." (Image credit: Chris Carlson)

items.title: European soccer leaders make plans to take Belgium's side against FIFA
items.standfirst: The continental confederation UEFA, of which the Royal Belgian Football Association is a member, is considering whether to issue a statement challenging a FIFA decision to suspend a red card.
items.content: FIFA’s decision to suspend Folarin Balogun’s one-match ban has sparked anger inside European soccer, with both continental confederation UEFA and the Royal Belgian Football Association discussing possible next steps, according to a high-ranking UEFA official. The official, who was granted anonymity to discuss ongoing deliberations, said no final decision had been taken. As of Sunday evening, UEFA’s plan was to release a statement on Monday in response to FIFA’s ruling, according to a person familiar with the matter. The statement is expected to support the Belgian association, which was directly affected after Balogun was permitted to play against Belgium despite having been sent off in the previous match. Belgium is a member of UEFA. “In order to safeguard the legitimate rights of all participating teams and to protect the fundamental principles of fair play in our sport, both at this FIFA World Cup and at future editions of the tournament, the RBFA is investigating all potential options,” the Belgian association said in a statement released after FIFA's ruling. FIFA's ruling has also prompted criticism from inside soccer's global governing body. One of the organization’s vice presidents, asked by POLITICO for his view of the Balogun case, described it in a WhatsApp message as an “utter disgrace.” The vice president was granted anonymity to speak candidly about the matter.

ai_summary: FIFA lets US forward Balogun play despite red card
Belgian soccer federation challenges the decision
UEFA criticizes FIFA's ruling as unjustifiable
European soccer leaders consider a statement
The decision has sparked widespread criticism

clusters.title: Russian strikes on Kyiv expose widening air defence gaps on eve of Nato summit
clusters.neutral_headline: Russian strikes hit Kyiv and kill many

items.title: Russian strikes on Kyiv expose widening air defence gaps on eve of Nato summit
items.standfirst: Second assault on capital and surrounding region within days leaves 19 dead and buildings heavily damagedEurope live – latest updatesA wave of Russian missiles and drones has struck Kyiv, killing 19 people and heavily damaging apartment blocks and other buildings, in an attack on the eve of a Nato summit in Turkey that has exposed widening gaps in Ukraine’s air defences.Thirteen people were killed in the capital, which was Russia’s main target, and 56 were injured, according to the city’s administrative head, Tymur Tkachenko. Another six people were killed in the wider Kyiv region and 21 were inured, according to Mykola Kalashnyk, the head of the regional administration, and other emergency officials. Continue reading...
items.content: Second assault on capital and surrounding region within days leaves 19 dead and buildings heavily damagedEurope live – latest updatesA wave of Russian missiles and drones has struck Kyiv, killing 19 people and heavily damaging apartment blocks and other buildings, in an attack on the eve of a Nato summit in Turkey that has exposed widening gaps in Ukraine’s air defences.Thirteen people were killed in the capital, which was Russia’s main target, and 56 were injured, according to the city’s administrative head, Tymur Tkachenko. Another six people were killed in the wider Kyiv region and 21 were inured, according to Mykola Kalashnyk, the head of the regional administration, and other emergency officials. Continue reading...

items.title: Russian missile and drone attack on Ukraine's capital kills at least 12
items.standfirst: Russia launched waves of missiles and drones at Kyiv early Monday, killing at least 12 people in an attack that exposed widening gaps in Ukraine's air defenses, authorities said.
items.content: Russia launched waves of missiles and drones at Kyiv early Monday, killing at least 12 people in an attack that exposed widening gaps in Ukraine's air defenses, authorities said.(Image credit: Danylo Antoniuk)

ai_summary: 19 people killed in Russian missile attack

Thirteen people killed in the capital city

clusters.title: Hamas announces dissolution of Gaza governing body

items.title: Hamas announces dissolution of Gaza governing body
items.standfirst: A Palestinian technocratic committee will take its place to manage the enclave's day-to-day governance.
items.content: A Palestinian technocratic committee will take its place to manage the enclave's day-to-day governance.

items.title: Dissolution of Hamas's Gaza governing body won't bring 'significant change of life'
items.standfirst: Hamas announced on July 6 the dissolution of the body that has governed the Gaza Strip for nearly two decades. The decision would clear the way for a technocratic committee to implement civilian rule. But questions remain. FRANCE 24's Noga Tarnopolsky tells us more from Jerusalem.
items.content: Hamas announced on July 6 the dissolution of the body that has governed the Gaza Strip for nearly two decades. The decision would clear the way for a technocratic committee to implement civilian rule. But questions remain. FRANCE 24's Noga Tarnopolsky tells us more from Jerusalem.

items.title: Hamas dissolves Gaza governing body, clearing way for technocratic committee
items.standfirst: The Palestinian movement Hamas announced Monday that it had dissolved its governing body in the Gaza Strip after nearly 20 years in power, paving the way for a technocratic committee to administer the territory.
items.content: The Palestinian movement Hamas announced Monday that it had dissolved its governing body in the Gaza Strip after nearly 20 years in power, paving the way for a technocratic committee to administer the territory.

clusters.title: At least 14 killed in Kyiv after second massive Russian attack
clusters.neutral_headline: Russian attack on Kyiv kills at least 14

items.title: At least 14 killed in Kyiv after second massive Russian attack
items.standfirst: Kyiv has been struck by Russian missiles for the second time in less than a week, with at least 14 people killed. While Russia says it hit military-industrial targets and energy sites, the morning strikes hit residential areas in the early hours of the morning. FRANCE 24's Gulliver Cragg tells us more from Odesa.
items.content: Kyiv has been struck by Russian missiles for the second time in less than a week, with at least 14 people killed. While Russia says it hit military-industrial targets and energy sites, the morning strikes hit residential areas in the early hours of the morning. FRANCE 24's Gulliver Cragg tells us more from Odesa.

items.title: Kyiv struck by second deadly Russian attack in less than a week
items.standfirst: Russia has launched another deadly barrage of strikes on Kyiv. According to Ukrainian authorities, at least fourteen people have been killed, just hours after President Volodomyr Zelensky warned another attack was imminent. Earlier on July 5 the city of Zaporizhzhia was also hit and Moscow claims it has captured the town of Kostyantynivka in the east of the country. Kyiv has dismissed the announcement.
items.content: Russia has launched another deadly barrage of strikes on Kyiv. According to Ukrainian authorities, at least fourteen people have been killed, just hours after President Volodomyr Zelensky warned another attack was imminent. Earlier on July 5 the city of Zaporizhzhia was also hit and Moscow claims it has captured the town of Kostyantynivka in the east of the country. Kyiv has dismissed the announcement.

items.title: Deadly Russian strikes hit Kyiv on eve of Trump trip to NATO summit

items.content: The attack, which Ukraine’s president had warned of hours earlier, comes days after a combined Russian assault killed at least 31 people in Kyiv.
